title: How Humans Are Like Bloodhounds and Bats
gist: An interview teaser with Richard Louv — who coined 'nature deficit disorder' — previews his new book Noticing, arguing humans have ~30 distinct animal senses and that 'bioenchantment' may counter eco-anxiety, amid growing evidence that time in nature benefits health.

insights.claim: Extensive evidence now suggests a walk in the woods benefits sleep, mood, cognitive function, the heart, and many other health measures, an area that has drawn intense research interest since Louv coined 'nature deficit disorder' two decades ago.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Growing evidence base supports nature/wellness as a durable consumer-health and outdoor-recreation theme.
insights.quote: a walk in the woods can have benefits for sleep, mood, cognitive function, the heart, and many other measures of health

insights.claim: Louv argues humans have at least 30 distinct animal senses, some we may not be aware of, and that a more intimate 'bioenchantment' relationship to nature may be an antidote to the eco-anxiety of our age.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Reframes the nature-connection conversation; cultural/wellness signal more than market.
insights.quote: humans have at least 30 distinct animal senses, some of which we may not even be aware that we possess
insights.locator: He says humans have at least 30 distinct animal senses
